# Lab 183: Managing Storage

## Resource Names

| Resource | Value |
|---|---|
| S3 Bucket | `lab183-storage` |
| S3 Folder Prefix | `lab183-files/` |
| Snapshot Description | `lab183-processor-snapshot` |
| IAM Role | `S3BucketAccess` |
| EBS Volume ID | `vol-0b222992521185d43` |
| Processor Instance ID | `i-0f6b0a528d9335b6e` |
| Command Host Instance ID | `i-0dcc375804fd51a3a` |
| Initial Snapshot ID | `snap-00006882d3afb4b3f` |
| S3 file1.txt VersionId | `xghJn4jRPsgw2BuRncCUO06MSVriQSfx` |
| AWS Region | US West (Oregon) us-west-2 |

---

## What I Did

This lab covered three things: creating and automating EBS snapshots, using a Python script to manage how many snapshots you keep, and syncing files between an EC2 instance and S3 with versioning enabled so you can recover deleted files. The environment was already set up with two EC2 instances in a VPC in US West Oregon — a Command Host (t3.medium, `i-0dcc375804fd51a3a`) and a Processor (t3.micro, `i-0f6b0a528d9335b6e`). I did everything through EC2 Instance Connect in the browser, no SSH client needed.

---

## Task 1: Creating and Configuring Resources

### Task 1.1: Create an S3 Bucket

Created the bucket `lab183-storage` in the S3 console. Region defaulted to US West Oregon. The console showed a green success banner straight away with creation timestamp March 25, 2026 at 14:05:18 UTC+1.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="S3 bucket lab183-storage created" width="800">


### Task 1.2: Attach Instance Profile to Processor

Went to EC2 > Instances, selected the Processor (`i-0f6b0a528d9335b6e`), then Actions > Security > Modify IAM role. Selected `S3BucketAccess` from the dropdown and clicked Update IAM role.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Modify IAM role S3BucketAccess" width="800">


---

## Task 2: Taking Snapshots of Your Instance

### Task 2.1: Connecting to the Command Host EC2 Instance

Connected to the Command Host via EC2 Instance Connect. The terminal opened at IP 10.5.0.75 with the Amazon Linux 2 banner. There was a note about AL2 end of life on 2026-06-30.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Command Host terminal connected" width="800">


### Task 2.2: Taking an Initial Snapshot

Retrieved the Processor's EBS volume ID and instance ID:

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Volume ID and Instance ID retrieved" width="800">


Stopped the Processor and waited. The `stop-instances` command returned JSON showing `CurrentState: stopping`. The `wait` command returned silently when done.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Stopping processor instance" width="800">


EC2 console confirmed the Processor was in Stopped state while the Command Host stayed Running.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Processor stopped in EC2 console" width="800">


Created the snapshot with description `lab183-processor-snapshot`. Output confirmed `SnapshotId: snap-00006882d3afb4b3f` with State `pending`.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="create-snapshot output pending" width="800">


After waiting, the EC2 Snapshots console showed the snapshot as Completed, 1.95 GiB, description `lab183-processor-snapshot`, started 2026/03/25 at 14:26 GM.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Snapshot completed in EC2 console" width="800">


### Task 2.3: Scheduling the Creation of Subsequent Snapshots

Created a cron job that runs every minute. Verified with `crontab -l`, then waited and ran `describe-snapshots --output table` to confirm snapshots were accumulating.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Multiple snapshots from cron job" width="800">


### Task 2.4: Retaining the Last Two Snapshots

Stopped the cron job with `crontab -r`. Before running the script, `describe-snapshots` returned 6 IDs. The script was visible with `more snapshotter_v2.py` — it sorts all snapshots by `start_time` and deletes all but the two most recent.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="6 snapshots before cleanup" width="800">


Ran `python3.8 snapshotter_v2.py`. It deleted 5 snapshots and left only `snap-0813f0177da3773d6` and `snap-0a26f1171c2b933f1` — the script had created a new one during its run and kept that plus the next most recent.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Only 2 snapshots after cleanup" width="800">


---

## Task 3: Challenge: Synchronize Files with Amazon S3

### Task 3.1: Downloading and Unzipping Sample Files

Opened a second EC2 Instance Connect session for the Processor at IP 10.5.0.21.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Processor terminal connected" width="800">


Downloaded files.zip (72,110 bytes at 38.3 MB/s in 0.002s) and unzipped it. All three files extracted cleanly.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="files.zip downloaded and unzipped" width="800">


### Task 3.2: Syncing Files

Enabled versioning on `lab183-storage` first — this is the step that makes file recovery possible. Then synced the files folder. All three files uploaded to `lab183-storage/lab183-files/`.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Versioning enabled and files synced to S3" width="800">


`s3 ls` confirmed all three files at 13:56:39 — file1.txt 30,318 bytes, file2.txt 43,784 bytes, file3.txt 96,675 bytes.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="All 3 files listed in S3" width="800">


Deleted file1.txt locally then ran `sync --delete`. Output confirmed `delete: s3://lab183-storage/lab183-files/file1.txt`. Only file2 and file3 remained in S3.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="file1.txt deleted from S3" width="800">


Ran `list-object-versions`. The Versions block showed VersionId `xghJn4jRPsgw2BuRncCUO06MSVriQSfx`, Size 30318, LastModified 2026-03-25T13:56:39.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="list-object-versions showing VersionId" width="800">


Downloaded the previous version with `get-object --version-id xghJn4jRPsgw2BuRncCUO06MSVriQSfx`. The response confirmed ContentLength 30318. `ls files` showed all three files restored locally.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="file1.txt restored locally" width="800">


Re-synced to push file1.txt back to S3. Final `s3 ls` showed all three files — file1.txt now at 14:04:31 as a new version.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="All 3 files back in S3" width="800">


---

## Challenges I Had

The first problem happened at the very start of Task 2.2 when running `create-snapshot`. I copied the command from a formatted document and the quote characters around the description were converted to typographic curly quotes. The CLI threw `Unknown options: lab183-processor-snapshot` because it could not parse the description value. The fix was to type the quotes manually as straight ASCII characters.

The second consistent issue throughout the lab was pasting too much text into the terminal. When you copy a command from a guide that includes explanatory text around it and paste the whole thing, the terminal tries to run each line as a command. This produces a wall of `bash: command not found` errors for every English word. Harmless but wastes time.

The versioning recovery in Task 3.2 had a specific trap. I ran `list-object-versions` when versioning had not been enabled before the first sync, so the output only showed `RequestCharged: null` and `Prefix` with nothing in the Versions block. Had to enable versioning, clear the S3 prefix, and redo the entire sync workflow from scratch before the Versions block appeared with anything to recover.

---

## What I Learned

The thing that stuck with me most from Task 2 is how `snapshotter_v2.py` works. It does not just delete old snapshots — it first creates a new one for every volume it finds, then sorts all existing snapshots by `start_time` and removes everything except the two most recent. So when I ran it with 6 snapshots, it created a 7th, then deleted 5, leaving 2. The two that remained were the brand new one it just created plus the next most recent.

For the S3 side, the key thing is the relationship between versioning and deletion. When versioning is on and you delete an object via `sync --delete`, S3 does not remove the data. It adds a delete marker on top. The file content is still there, still accessible through `get-object` with the right VersionId. To actually remove data permanently you would need to explicitly delete each version and delete marker individually.

The other practical thing I learned is that `s3 sync` without `--delete` is always additive — it will upload new and changed files but never remove anything from the destination. The `--delete` flag is what makes it truly mirror the source. Understanding when to use each mode matters because running `sync --delete` when you did not intend to can silently remove a lot of files from S3.

---

## Commands Reference

All CLI commands are in `commands.sh`.
